title: Calzone
subtitle: Folded Neapolitan pizza with ricotta, mozzarella, and salami
excerpt: A pizza dough folded over a filling of ricotta, mozzarella, and salami, sealed at the edge and baked until the outside is golden and the inside is a molten, steaming pocket. The classic Neapolitan calzone uses ricotta as the base rather than tomato sauce.
sourceNotes: Calzone is a Neapolitan pizza variant documented in Italian cookery literature from the nineteenth century. The name derives from the Neapolitan dialect word for trouser-leg, referring to the folded, half-moon shape. The ricotta-and-salami filling is the Neapolitan standard.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough needs 2 to 4 hours at room temperature, or 24 hours in the fridge.

content.text: The calzone is a pizza folded in half. The filling goes on one side of the disc; the other half folds over and is crimped at the edge to seal. The ricotta filling needs to be well seasoned — it absorbs the salt during baking, and an under-seasoned filling will taste bland regardless of how good the dough is. Drain the ricotta in a sieve for 30 minutes before using; excess moisture turns the filling watery.

Cut a small vent in the top to let steam escape. Without a vent, the calzone puffs up violently during baking and can burst unpredictably. Rest for 5 minutes before cutting — the filling inside is very hot.

items.prepNote: drained in a sieve for 30 minutes

content.text: Dissolve the {{yeast-fast-action}} of yeast in the {{water}} of lukewarm water. Add the {{00-flour}} of flour, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Divide into 4 balls, cover, and rest at room temperature for 2 to 3 hours.

Combine the drained {{ricotta}} of ricotta, {{parmesan}} of Parmesan, and a generous amount of salt and pepper in a bowl. Mix well. Set aside the {{mozzarella}} mozzarella and {{chorizo}} of sliced chorizo.

Heat the oven to 230°C (fan 210°C) with a baking tray inside. On a floured surface, roll each dough ball out to a 28 cm round. Spoon the ricotta filling over one half, leaving a 2 cm border at the edge. Top with the mozzarella and chorizo. Fold the bare half of the dough over the filling. Crimp the edge by pressing and folding to seal tightly. Brush the top with the beaten {{eggs}} egg. Cut a small vent in the top. Slide onto the hot baking tray and bake for 20 to 25 minutes until deep golden. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Filling has leaked through the seal
items.cause: Edge not sealed tightly, or filling too wet
items.fix: Press the edge firmly and fold it over itself; drain the ricotta well before using; do not overfill

items.symptom: Calzone has burst at the top
items.cause: No vent cut, or vent too small
items.fix: Cut a vent at least 2 cm long in the top before baking; steam needs a clear escape route
Preheat the baking tray in the oven for at least 20 minutes; slide the calzone directly onto the hot tray

content.text: The calzone is the pizza in a different form: the same dough, the same filling philosophy, the same high-heat baking. In Naples it is sold as a street food, fried in oil (pizza fritta) or baked, and eaten folded in half like a book. The ricotta filling is the Neapolitan default; northern Italian versions use more cured meat and less dairy. Outside Italy the calzone is often associated with Americanised pizza chains, which is a misrepresentation — the original is lighter and less heavy than its chain-restaurant version.
